original_abstract: Abstract In the context of high-dimensional credit card fraud data, researchers and practitioners commonly utilize feature selection techniques to enhance the performance of fraud detection models. This study presents a comparison in model performance using the most important features selected by SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and the model’s built-in feature importance list. Both methods rank features and choose the most significant ones for model assessment. To evaluate the effectiveness of these feature selection techniques, classification models are built using five classifiers: XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Random Forest. The Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) serves as the evaluation metric. All experiments are executed on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset. The experimental outcomes and statistical tests indicate that feature selection methods based on importance values outperform those based on SHAP values across classifiers and various feature subset sizes. For models trained on larger datasets, it is recommended to use the model’s built-in feature importance list as the primary feature selection method over SHAP. This suggestion is based on the rationale that computing SHAP feature importance is a distinct activity, while models naturally provide built-in feature importance as part of the training process, requiring no additional effort. Consequently, opting for the model’s built-in feature importance list can offer a more efficient and practical approach for larger datasets and more intricate models.
humanized_abstract: Abstract High-dimensional credit card fraud data is a common case in fraud detection using machine learning. Feature selection methods are widely used to improve the performance of the models. In this work, we investigate the performance of classification models using the features selected based on SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and the model’s feature importance list. We trained five different types of classifiers: XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees and Random Forest on the features selected based on the two methods and observed the performance of the models using the Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) metric. The experiments were conducted on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset. The results and statistical tests show that the feature selection methods based on the importance values outperform the SHAP values for all classifiers and for all feature subset sizes. Using model’s feature importance list in the features are selected for large datasets and hence it is recommended to use the model’s feature importance list than the SHAP values as calculating the SHAP feature importance is a separate task and the model’s feature importance list is available without any extra effort. Hence, for larger models and datasets, the model’s feature importance list will be more efficient.
